content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 22

**Chapter:** Leviticus 22 (33 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Sources:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Total Disagreements:** 58

---

## Format

Each entry records: **Verse | Hebrew term/phrase | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Consensus ruling | Basis**

---

## Disagreements

### V. 1 — Name form: Moses

- **EMET:** "Moshe"
- **LOGOS:** "Moses"
- **PNEUMA:** "Moses"
- **Consensus:** "Moses"
- **Basis:** English name form per project convention. EMET's transliteration is appropriate for hyper-literal analysis but not for the consensus text. LOGOS/PNEUMA follow the established project standard.

---

### V. 2 — Verb: yinnazeru (Nifal of n-z-r)

- **EMET:** "they shall separate themselves [ve-yinnazeru]"
- **LOGOS:** "that they separate themselves"
- **PNEUMA:** "to handle the sacred offerings with care"
- **Consensus:** "that they separate themselves"
- **Basis:** The Nifal of *n-z-r* (the nazir-root) denotes deliberate, consecrated withdrawal from holy things when ritually unqualified — not merely general care. PNEUMA's "handle with care" flattens the root into a practical caution, losing the theological resonance of the same root that gives us *nazir* (Nazirite) and *nezer* (crown). EMET and LOGOS correctly render the Nifal reflexive. The slight difference between EMET and LOGOS is word-order/syntax (EMET: declarative "they shall"; LOGOS: purpose clause "that they"); LOGOS's purpose-clause structure fits the verse's instruction more naturally.

---

### V. 2 — Name form: Aaron

- **EMET:** "Aharon"
- **LOGOS:** "Aaron"
- **PNEUMA:** "Aaron"
- **Consensus:** "Aaron"
- **Basis:** English name form per project convention. EMET's "Aharon" is a transliteration, not the established consensus rendering.

---

### V. 2 — Object of separation: "holy things of the sons of Israel" vs. "sacred offerings of the Israelites"

- **EMET:** "the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el [qodshei benei-Yisra'el]"
- **LOGOS:** "the holy things of the sons of Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "the sacred offerings of the Israelites"
- **Consensus:** "the holy things of the sons of Israel"
- **Basis:** *Qodshei benei-Yisra'el* = "the holy things of the sons of Israel." PNEUMA narrows this to "sacred offerings," which implies only actively presented offerings, whereas *qodshim* encompasses all categories of holy portions (grain offerings, peace offerings, firstfruits, etc.) that devolve to the priests. "The holy things" is broader and more accurate. "Sons of Israel" is the project's standard rendering for *bney yisrael* (vs. PNEUMA's "Israelites").

---

### V. 2 — Verse structure: two-clause vs. one-clause

- **EMET:** Preserves two-clause structure: separation from holy things + not profaning the name
- **LOGOS:** Preserves two-clause structure
- **PNEUMA:** Compresses into one flowing sentence, losing the syntactic division
- **Consensus:** Two-clause structure (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Basis:** The MT's verse has two distinct commands: (a) separate from the holy things; (b) do not profane the holy name. The verse functions as a topic sentence for the chapter, and its dual scope must be visible in the translation. PNEUMA's compression obscures this.

---

### V. 3 — "Throughout your generations" — position and scope

- **EMET:** "any man of all your offspring throughout their generations [le-dorotam]"
- **LOGOS:** "Throughout your generations, if any man of all your offspring"
- **PNEUMA:** "For all your future generations, if any of your descendants"
- **Consensus:** "Throughout your generations, any man of all your offspring"
- **Basis:** *Le-dorotam* = "throughout their generations" — all generations past and future. PNEUMA's "future generations" restricts the scope to what is to come, whereas the MT's sense is the totality of the priestly lineage across all time. LOGOS's fronting of "Throughout your generations" is the most natural English placement.

---

### V. 3 — Penalty formula: "cut off from before me"

- **EMET:** "shall be cut off from before my face [mi-lefanai]"
- **LOGOS:** "shall be cut off from before me"
- **PNEUMA:** "must be cut off from my presence"
- **Consensus:** "shall be cut off from before me"
- **Basis:** The MT's *mi-lefanai* is "from before my face" — a relational, directional formula emphasizing exclusion from the divine presence in its covenantal-positional sense. LOGOS's "from before me" preserves this. PNEUMA's "from my presence" is an acceptable paraphrase but loses the directionality of *mi-lefanai* (from *before*). EMET's "my face" is the most literal but slightly awkward in English for the karet formula.

---

### V. 4 — "Leper" vs. "skin-diseased person" vs. "who has a skin disease"

- **EMET:** "skin-diseased person [tsaru'a]"
- **LOGOS:** "leper"
- **PNEUMA:** "who has a skin disease"
- **Consensus:** "leper"
- **Basis:** "Leper" is the established formal translation tradition (ESV, NASB, KJV) for *tsaru'a* throughout Leviticus 13–14 and in the legal-status contexts of Leviticus 22. While EMET correctly notes that *tsara'at* encompasses a broader range of skin conditions than modern leprosy (Hansen's disease), the consensus adopts the longstanding rendering for consistency across the Leviticus series. PNEUMA's "who has a skin disease" is the most clinically precise but least formal.

---

### V. 4 — "Unclean by/from a corpse" — tame-nefesh

- **EMET:** "anything unclean by a dead body [tame-nefesh]"
- **LOGOS:** "anything made unclean from a corpse"
- **PNEUMA:** "something made unclean by a corpse"
- **Consensus:** "anything made unclean by a corpse"
- **Basis:** *Tame-nefesh* = "unclean of soul/corpse" — the technical term for corpse-impurity. All three capture the sense; "made unclean by a corpse" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) best renders the passive participial sense. EMET's "dead body" is slightly less precise than "corpse" for the priestly term *nefesh* in this technical use.

---

### V. 4 — "Emission of semen" vs. "discharge of seed" vs. "seminal emission"

- **EMET:** "a discharge of seed [shich'vat-zara]"
- **LOGOS:** "an emission of semen"
- **PNEUMA:** "a seminal emission"
- **Consensus:** "an emission of semen"
- **Basis:** *Shich'vat-zara* = "a lying of seed" — seminal emission (Lev 15:16–18). LOGOS's "emission of semen" is the formal standard (ESV). PNEUMA's "seminal emission" transposes the noun-verb relationship. EMET's "discharge of seed" uses "seed" — accurate but less conventional medically. "Emission of semen" is preferred as the formal equivalent.

---

### V. 5 — "Swarming thing" vs. "swarming creature"

- **EMET:** "swarming thing"
- **LOGOS:** "swarming thing"
- **PNEUMA:** "swarming creature"
- **Consensus:** "swarming thing"
- **Basis:** *Sherets* is the established project convention "swarming thing" (cf. Lev 11). PNEUMA's "swarming creature" is equivalent but not the project standard.

---

### V. 5 — Structure of the contamination clause

- **EMET:** "in a man who makes him unclean by any uncleanness of his"
- **LOGOS:** "any person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness"
- **PNEUMA:** "touches a person whose uncleanness — whatever it may be — makes him unclean"
- **Consensus:** "a person by whom he would be made unclean — whatever his uncleanness"
- **Basis:** The MT's *o be-adam asher yetamme-lo le-khol tumato* is "or in a person who makes him unclean by any of his uncleanness." LOGOS renders the agent (*adam* = "person") and the scope (*le-khol tumato* = "whatever his uncleanness") clearly. EMET's "in a man" for *be-adam* is literal but awkward. PNEUMA restructures the clause unnecessarily. The dash in LOGOS's rendering effectively preserves the MT's appositional expansion.

---

### V. 6 — "Bathe his flesh/body in water"

- **EMET:** "bathed his flesh [besaro] in water"
- **LOGOS:** "bathed his body in water"
- **PNEUMA:** "bathed himself in water"
- **Consensus:** "bathed his body in water"
- **Basis:** *Besaro* = "his flesh/body." LOGOS's "his body" is the natural English equivalent for the ritual immersion in this legal context. EMET's "his flesh" is accurate but slightly archaic for English readers. PNEUMA's "himself" drops the bodily specificity of *basar* present in the MT.

---

### V. 7 — "The sun goes down" vs. "the sun sets"

- **EMET:** "when the sun sets"
- **LOGOS:** "When the sun goes down"
- **PNEUMA:** "When the sun has set"
- **Consensus:** "When the sun goes down"
- **Basis:** *U-va ha-shemesh* = "when the sun comes/goes" — its setting. LOGOS's "goes down" is natural English. EMET's "sets" is equally standard. PNEUMA's past perfect "has set" implies completed action rather than the conditional boundary the verse establishes. "Goes down" is preferred as the standard formal rendering.

---

### V. 7 — "For they are his food" vs. "for it is his food"

- **EMET:** "for it is his food [ki lachmo hu]"
- **LOGOS:** "for they are his food"
- **PNEUMA:** Omits the clause entirely
- **Consensus:** "for they are his food"
- **Basis:** The MT's *ki lachmo hu* ("for his bread/food it is") is a warrant clause — explaining *why* the priest is allowed to eat: it is his livelihood, his portion. PNEUMA's omission of this entire rationale clause is inadmissible. Both EMET and LOGOS preserve it. LOGOS's plural "they are" is preferred as the antecedent (the holy things) is plural. EMET's singular "it is" treats the collective as a unit, which is also defensible.

---

### V. 8 — "What dies of itself" vs. "a carcass [nevelah]"

- **EMET:** "A carcass [nevelah]"
- **LOGOS:** "what dies of itself"
- **PNEUMA:** "anything found dead"
- **Consensus:** "what dies of itself"
- **Basis:** *Nevelah* is an animal that died without proper slaughter. LOGOS's "what dies of itself" (ESV, NASB) is the established formal rendering that preserves the specific meaning: natural or unslaughtered death. EMET's "carcass" is broader — applicable to any dead animal including properly slaughtered ones. PNEUMA's "found dead" is too passive, implying only discovery rather than the mode of death.

---

### V. 8 — "What is torn by beasts" vs. "one torn [terefah]"

- **EMET:** "one torn [terefah]"
- **LOGOS:** "what is torn by beasts"
- **PNEUMA:** "torn by wild animals"
- **Consensus:** "what is torn by beasts"
- **Basis:** *Terefah* (from *taraf*, "to tear") denotes an animal killed or mortally wounded by predators. LOGOS's "what is torn by beasts" makes the agent explicit and is the standard rendering (ESV: "torn by beasts"). EMET's "one torn" is literal but drops the agent. PNEUMA's "wild animals" is equivalent to LOGOS's "beasts."

---

### V. 8 — "Making himself unclean by it" — the purpose clause

- **EMET:** "to make himself unclean by it [la-tame-vah bo]"
- **LOGOS:** "becoming unclean by it"
- **PNEUMA:** Omits this clause
- **Consensus:** "making himself unclean by it"
- **Basis:** The MT's *la-tame-vah bo* is a purposive/result infinitive: eating such things makes the priest unclean. Both EMET and LOGOS preserve it. PNEUMA's omission of this clause loses the explanation of why the nevelah/terefah prohibition applies specifically to the priest (the effect of self-defilement). The participial "making himself unclean" flows naturally.

---

### V. 9 — "They shall keep my charge" — opening conjunction and subject

- **EMET:** "And they shall keep my charge"
- **LOGOS:** "They shall therefore keep my charge"
- **PNEUMA:** "The priests must keep my charge"
- **Consensus:** "They shall keep my charge"
- **Basis:** LOGOS adds "therefore" as a logical connector not present in the MT's *ve-shamru et-mishmarti*. While the connection is real, the MT's direct statement does not include a connector. PNEUMA's explicit "The priests" is an addition beyond what the MT's implicit subject requires. The simple "They shall keep" follows the MT. The *vav* ("and") is present but becomes implicit in the transition from vv.4–8.

---

### V. 9 — "Lest they bear sin" vs. "and not bear sin"

- **EMET:** "and they shall not bear sin for it [ve-lo yis'u alav chet]"
- **LOGOS:** "lest they bear sin for it"
- **PNEUMA:** "or they will bear the sin"
- **Consensus:** "lest they bear sin for it"
- **Basis:** The MT's *ve-lo yis'u* functions as a purpose/consequence clause: "keep my charge [so that they will] not bear sin." LOGOS's "lest they bear sin" captures this purpose-clause force. EMET's "and they shall not bear sin" reads as an additional parallel commandment rather than a consequence of keeping the charge. PNEUMA's "or they will bear the sin" reverses the construction into a negative alternative and adds the definite article ("the sin") not in the MT.

---

### V. 10 — "Sojourner of the priest's household" vs. "tenant of a priest"

- **EMET:** "a tenant of a priest [toshav kohen]"
- **LOGOS:** "sojourner of the priest's household"
- **PNEUMA:** "No guest staying with a priest"
- **Consensus:** "sojourner of the priest's household"
- **Basis:** *Toshav* is a long-term resident alien in the household, not merely a guest or tenant. LOGOS's "sojourner of the priest's household" preserves the established *toshav* rendering (ESV: "sojourner") and specifies the priestly household context. EMET's "tenant" introduces a property-rental connotation. PNEUMA's "guest" understates the residential stability of *toshav*. "Sojourner" is the project standard for this term.

---

### V. 10 — "Hired servant" vs. "hired worker"

- **EMET:** "hired worker [sachir]"
- **LOGOS:** "hired servant"
- **PNEUMA:** "hired worker"
- **Consensus:** "hired servant"
- **Basis:** *Sachir* is a hired laborer. "Hired servant" (LOGOS, ESV) is the formal legal register term in the project's vocabulary for this type of contractual relationship. "Hired worker" (EMET/PNEUMA) is equivalent but slightly less formal for the legal context.

---

### V. 11 — "Buys a person" vs. "buys a slave"

- **EMET:** "buys a person [nefesh] with his silver"
- **LOGOS:** "buys a slave as his property with money"
- **PNEUMA:** "buys a slave with his own money"
- **Consensus:** "buys a person with his money"
- **Basis:** The MT's *ve-kohen ki-yiqneh nefesh qinyan kaspo* uses *nefesh* ("person/soul"), not a specific word for slave. LOGOS and PNEUMA both add "slave" as an interpretive gloss, which is accurate but goes beyond the MT's lexical choice. EMET preserves "person." The consensus uses "a person" while the context (slave-purchase) is clear from "buys... with his money." LOGOS's additional "as his property" is also an expansion.

---

### V. 11 — "Those born in his house" vs. "one born of his house"

- **EMET:** "one born of his house [ylid beito]"
- **LOGOS:** "those who are born in his house"
- **PNEUMA:** "a slave is born in his household" (adding "slave" explicitly)
- **Consensus:** "those born in his house"
- **Basis:** *Ylid beito* = "one born of his house" — the house-born slave. LOGOS's plural "those" is appropriate for the general category. PNEUMA's explicit "slave" is an addition. EMET's singular "one born" is more literal for the singular Hebrew but the plural "those" is clearer for the legal context covering a whole category.

---

### V. 11 — "Of his food" vs. "of his bread" / "his food"

- **EMET:** "of his bread [lachmo]"
- **LOGOS:** "of his food"
- **PNEUMA:** "his food" (no partitive)
- **Consensus:** "of his food"
- **Basis:** *Be-lachmo* = "of his bread/food." "His food" (LOGOS) is the natural English equivalent for *lechem* in general food-reference contexts. EMET's "his bread" is literal but slightly archaic. PNEUMA drops the partitive *be-* ("of"). "Of his food" (LOGOS) preserves both the sense and the partitive.

---

### V. 12 — "Marries an outsider" vs. "becomes the wife of an outsider"

- **EMET:** "becomes the wife of an outsider [le-ish zar]"
- **LOGOS:** "marries an outsider"
- **PNEUMA:** "marries an outsider"
- **Consensus:** "marries an outsider"
- **Basis:** *Ki tihyeh le-ish zar* = "when she comes to belong to a man who is an outsider" — idiomatic Hebrew for marriage. "Marries an outsider" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the natural English equivalent. EMET's "becomes the wife of" is accurate but more circumlocutory.

---

### V. 13 — "Has no offspring" vs. "has no children"

- **EMET:** "has no offspring [zera]"
- **LOGOS:** "has no children"
- **PNEUMA:** "with no children"
- **Consensus:** "has no offspring"
- **Basis:** *Ve-zera ein lah* = "and seed/offspring there is not to her." *Zera* encompasses all descendants. "Offspring" (EMET) preserves the legal scope of the MT's term more accurately than "children," which in modern English suggests minor-age dependents. The childlessness condition determines the woman's household-status, and "offspring" captures the broader legal sense.

---

### V. 14 — "Inadvertently" vs. "by mistake"

- **EMET:** "inadvertently [bi-shgagah]"
- **LOGOS:** "inadvertently"
- **PNEUMA:** "by mistake"
- **Consensus:** "inadvertently"
- **Basis:** *Bi-shgagah* is the standard Levitical legal term for unintentional violation (cf. Lev 4:2; 5:15). "Inadvertently" (ESV, NASB) is the established formal rendering and matches the priestly legal register. PNEUMA's "by mistake" is acceptable colloquially but less precise for the legal formula.

---

### V. 14 — "Add a fifth of its value" vs. "add its fifth"

- **EMET:** "add its fifth [chamishito] to it"
- **LOGOS:** "add a fifth of its value to it"
- **PNEUMA:** "replacing it with something equivalent and adding a fifth of its value on top"
- **Consensus:** "add a fifth of its value to it"
- **Basis:** *Ve-yasaf chamishito alav* = "and he shall add its fifth to it." LOGOS's "a fifth of its value" makes the economic calculation explicit and natural. EMET's "its fifth" is literal and compact. PNEUMA's "replacing it with something equivalent" is an expansion: the MT's verse simply says add the fifth and give the holy thing to the priest. The MT does not explicitly state "replacing with something equivalent" at this verse (the principal restitution is implied by "give the holy thing to the priest"). PNEUMA's expansion goes beyond the MT's plain statement.

---

### V. 15 — Clause structure and scope

- **EMET:** "And they shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el which they contribute [asher yarimu] to the LORD"
- **LOGOS:** "They shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Israel, which they contribute to the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** "The priests must not allow the sacred offerings of the Israelites to be treated as common things"
- **Consensus:** "They shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Israel, which they contribute to the LORD"
- **Basis:** PNEUMA's "treated as common things" is an interpretive paraphrase of *yechalelu* ("profane"); "profane" is the chapter's key technical term and must be preserved. PNEUMA also omits the relative clause "which they contribute to the LORD" (*asher yarimu la-YHWH*), which specifies the category of holy things in view (the elevated contributions). EMET/LOGOS preserve both elements.

---

### V. 16 — "Bear iniquity and guilt" — avon ashmah

- **EMET:** "cause them to bear the iniquity of guilt [avon ashmah]"
- **LOGOS:** "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt"
- **PNEUMA:** "bring guilt upon themselves"
- **Consensus:** "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt"
- **Basis:** *Avon ashmah* is a compound of *avon* ("iniquity/culpable act") and *ashmah* ("guilt/liability"). LOGOS's "iniquity and guilt" preserves both terms. EMET's "iniquity of guilt" treats it as a construct chain, which is grammatically possible but slightly awkward. PNEUMA collapses both terms into "guilt" and shifts the subject and the verb ("bring... upon themselves" vs. the MT's "cause them to bear"). "Bear iniquity and guilt" is the clearest.

---

### V. 17 — Structural: separate divine speech formula

- **EMET:** Renders v.17 as a separate verse: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:"
- **LOGOS:** Renders v.17 as a separate verse
- **PNEUMA:** Collapses vv.1 and 17 into a single discourse unit, noting "The LORD spoke to Moses:" at v.1 and v.17 without full verse separation
- **Consensus:** Full separate verse rendering (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Basis:** The threefold *vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe* structure (vv.1, 17, 26) is the chapter's literary skeleton — each introduction marks a new legal block. These must be rendered as distinct verse markers, preserving the chapter's three-panel structure.

---

### V. 18 — "Sojourners in Israel" vs. "any foreigner living in Israel"

- **EMET:** "sojourners [ha-ger] in Yisra'el"
- **LOGOS:** "sojourners in Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "any foreigner living in Israel"
- **Consensus:** "sojourners in Israel"
- **Basis:** *Ha-ger* in v.18 is the resident alien — the integrated *ger* who may bring offerings, distinct from the *ben-nekhar* (true foreigner) in v.25 who may not. PNEUMA's "foreigner" conflates these two categories, blurring a distinction the chapter carefully maintains. "Sojourner" (EMET/LOGOS) is the project standard for *ger*.

---

### V. 18 — "In fulfillment of a vow" vs. "for any of their vows"

- **EMET:** "for any of their vows or for any of their freewill offerings"
- **LOGOS:** "in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering"
- **PNEUMA:** "whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering"
- **Consensus:** "whether in fulfillment of a vow or as a freewill offering"
- **Basis:** EMET's plural "their vows... their freewill offerings" is more literal for the MT's plural forms (*nidreihem, nidvotam*). LOGOS/PNEUMA use singular categories. The consensus adopts a singular/generic formulation for naturalness. "In fulfillment of a vow" (LOGOS) better captures the *le-fallei neder* sense than EMET's plain "for any of their vows."

---

### V. 19 — "For your acceptance, it shall be" vs. "to be accepted you shall offer"

- **EMET:** "for your acceptance [li-retsone'khem] a male without blemish"
- **LOGOS:** "to be accepted you shall offer a male without blemish"
- **PNEUMA:** "you must present a male without defect... if you want it accepted"
- **Consensus:** "for your acceptance, it shall be a male without blemish"
- **Basis:** The MT's *li-retsone'khem zachar tamim* places the acceptance-purpose before the content specification. EMET preserves this word order; LOGOS inverts it. PNEUMA's "if you want it accepted" converts a requirement into a conditional. The *ratson*-formula is the governing theological criterion and should appear first as in the MT.

---

### V. 19 — "Without blemish" vs. "without defect"

- **EMET:** "without blemish [tamim]"
- **LOGOS:** "without blemish"
- **PNEUMA:** "without defect"
- **Consensus:** "without blemish"
- **Basis:** *Tamim* in sacrificial contexts is the established "without blemish" throughout the project (ESV, NASB, KJV). "Without blemish" preserves the verbal link to the priestly blemish (*mum*) vocabulary of chapter 21, where the priest's required bodily integrity and the animal's required quality are deliberately parallel. PNEUMA's "without defect" (NIV) breaks this terminological link.

---

### V. 20 — "For it will not be accepted for you" — ratson formula

- **EMET:** "for it will not be for acceptance for you"
- **LOGOS:** "for it will not be accepted for you"
- **PNEUMA:** "because it will not be accepted on your behalf"
- **Consensus:** "for it will not be accepted for you"
- **Basis:** *Ki lo le-ratson yihyeh lakhem* = "for it will not be for favor/acceptance for you." LOGOS's rendering is the most natural. EMET's "for acceptance" is more literal for *ratson*. PNEUMA's "on your behalf" for *lakhem* is a slight paraphrase. "For you" is the literal *lakhem*. LOGOS is preferred.

---

### V. 21 — "Peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offering"

- **EMET:** "peace offering [zevach shelamim]"
- **LOGOS:** "peace offerings"
- **PNEUMA:** "fellowship offering"
- **Consensus:** "peace offerings"
- **Basis:** *Zevach shelamim* = "sacrifice of peace offerings" — the established formal rendering throughout Leviticus (ESV, NASB). PNEUMA's "fellowship offering" (NIV) is interpretive, emphasizing the communal meal aspect of the *shelamim*. The project convention is "peace offerings" for *shelamim*.

---

### V. 21 — "To fulfill a vow" vs. "to make extraordinary a vow" [le-fallei neder]

- **EMET:** "to fulfill a vow [le-fallei neder]" (with note on the *pala* root's "extraordinary" sense)
- **LOGOS:** "to fulfill a vow"
- **PNEUMA:** "to fulfill a special vow"
- **Consensus:** "to fulfill a vow"
- **Basis:** *Le-fallei neder* is from *pala* ("to make extraordinary/distinct/set apart"). PNEUMA's "special vow" reflects the *pala* nuance but adds a qualifier beyond the MT's plain *neder*. The consensus preserves "a vow" without the qualifier; the *pala* nuance is recorded in the notes.

---

### V. 22 — "Broken" vs. "injured" for shavur

- **EMET:** "broken [shavur]"
- **LOGOS:** "broken"
- **PNEUMA:** "injured"
- **Consensus:** "broken"
- **Basis:** *Shavur* = "broken/fractured." PNEUMA's "injured" is too general. "Broken" (EMET/LOGOS) correctly renders the specific fracture sense of *shavur*.

---

### V. 22 — "Maimed" for charutz — three-way agreement with different notes

- **EMET:** "maimed or having a running sore [charutz]" (EMET notes: "incised/gashed/slit")
- **LOGOS:** "maimed"
- **PNEUMA:** "maimed"
- **Consensus:** "maimed"
- **Basis:** *Charutz* is a difficult term. The standard formal translation "maimed" (ESV, NASB) is preferred over EMET's more speculative "having a running sore" or "gashed/slit." All agree on "maimed" for the consensus text. EMET's note on the uncertain etymology is recorded here.

---

### V. 22 — "Running sore" vs. "discharge" for yabbelet

- **EMET:** "having a running sore [yabbelet]"
- **LOGOS:** "having a discharge"
- **PNEUMA:** "running sores"
- **Consensus:** "having a running sore"
- **Basis:** *Yabbelet* is a wart, growth, or running sore — a surface skin condition. LOGOS's "discharge" risks conflation with *zov* (the bodily discharge of Lev 15). "Running sore" (EMET/PNEUMA) better captures the skin-surface eruption or growth sense of *yabbelet*.

---

### V. 22 — "Rash" vs. "itch" vs. "scabby" for garav

- **EMET:** "scabby [garav]"
- **LOGOS:** "an itch"
- **PNEUMA:** "a rash"
- **Consensus:** "a rash"
- **Basis:** *Garav* is a skin eruption/scabbing condition (same term in the priestly blemish list, Lev 21:20). "Rash" (PNEUMA) is the most natural modern English for a surface skin eruption. LOGOS's "itch" is symptom-specific and too narrow. EMET's "scabby" is descriptive but reads as an adjective rather than a condition-noun.

---

### V. 22 — "Scabs" vs. "skin discharge" for yalefet

- **EMET:** "with a skin discharge [yalefet]"
- **LOGOS:** "scabs"
- **PNEUMA:** "festering skin"
- **Consensus:** "scabs"
- **Basis:** *Yalefet* (also Lev 21:20) is a dry, crusty skin condition. "Scabs" (LOGOS) is the most natural English rendering. EMET's "skin discharge" overreads *yalefet* toward a liquid discharge sense. PNEUMA's "festering skin" implies active suppuration.

---

### V. 22 — "Food offering" vs. "fire-offering" for isheh

- **EMET:** "a fire-offering [isheh]"
- **LOGOS:** "a food offering"
- **PNEUMA:** "a food offering"
- **Consensus:** "a food offering"
- **Basis:** *Isheh* (from *esh*, "fire") has traditionally been rendered "fire-offering." Modern formal translations (ESV, NASB) use "food offering" to capture the broader sense of the offering presented to the LORD. This rendering is adopted in the consensus for consistency with the project's formal equivalent choices throughout Leviticus. The note records the etymology.

---

### V. 23 — "Bull or a lamb" vs. "ox or a sheep"

- **EMET:** "an ox [shor] or a sheep [seh]"
- **LOGOS:** "A bull or a lamb"
- **PNEUMA:** "a bull or lamb"
- **Consensus:** "A bull or a lamb"
- **Basis:** *Shor* = "bull/ox" (a mature bovine male). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "bull" is preferred over EMET's "ox" for *shor* in this sacrifice context. *Seh* = "sheep/lamb"; "lamb" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the more conventional formal rendering for *seh* in sacrifice legislation (ESV).

---

### V. 23 — "Limb too long or too short" vs. "one limb extended or contracted"

- **EMET:** "one limb extended [sarua] or contracted [qalutt]"
- **LOGOS:** "a limb too long or too short"
- **PNEUMA:** "a limb too long or too short"
- **Consensus:** "a limb too long or too short"
- **Basis:** *Sarua ve-qalutt* describes proportional limb asymmetry. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "too long or too short" (ESV) is the most accessible and well-attested English rendering. EMET's "extended or contracted" is more literal but less natural in English. The note records the LXX's different interpretation (*monokoton kai kolobon*).

---

### V. 24 — "Testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut" vs. "damaged, crushed, torn, or cut genitals"

- **EMET:** "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut [machutz u-kathit u-nathiq ve-kharet]"
- **LOGOS:** "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut"
- **PNEUMA:** "with damaged, crushed, torn, or cut genitals"
- **Consensus:** "has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut"
- **Basis:** The MT's four terms (*machutz, kathit, nathiq, kharet*) describe a spectrum of testicular injury. EMET/LOGOS render all four precisely. PNEUMA's "damaged" for *machutz* ("bruised/compressed") is vaguer; PNEUMA's "genitals" generalizes what the MT specifies as testicles. The specific anatomical rendering is preferred.

---

### V. 25 — "Obtained from a foreigner" vs. "from the hand of a foreigner's son"

- **EMET:** "from the hand of a foreigner's son [ben-nekhar]"
- **LOGOS:** "animals obtained from a foreigner"
- **PNEUMA:** "such animals from a foreigner"
- **Consensus:** "any such animals obtained from a foreigner"
- **Basis:** *Mi-yad ben-nekhar* = "from the hand of a son of a foreigner." LOGOS's "obtained from a foreigner" captures the transactional sense (*mi-yad* = "from the hand of") without EMET's slightly awkward "foreigner's son" (the *ben-nekhar* is a standard biblical term for a non-Israelite non-resident, not literally a "foreigner's son"). The consensus adds "any such" to reflect *mi-kol-elleh* ("from any of these").

---

### V. 25 — "Their corruption is in them" / "there is a blemish in them" — two-clause structure

- **EMET:** "for their corruption is in them [mashchatam bahem] — a blemish is in them [mum bam]"
- **LOGOS:** "Since there is a blemish in them, because of their deformity"
- **PNEUMA:** "Since they are corrupt — they have a defect"
- **Consensus:** "for their corruption is in them — there is a blemish in them"
- **Basis:** The MT has two distinct parallel clauses: *ki mashchatam bahem* ("for their corruption/ruin is in them") and *mum bam* ("a blemish in them"). EMET preserves both. LOGOS reverses their order and renders *mashchatam* as "deformity" (too mild — *mashchat* from *shahat*, "to destroy/ruin," implies a more fundamental defect). PNEUMA's "corrupt" and "defect" preserve both ideas but restructure. The consensus adopts EMET's order and "corruption" for *mashchat*.

---

### V. 27 — "A bull or a sheep or a goat" vs. "a calf, a lamb, or a goat"

- **EMET:** "an ox or a sheep or a goat [shor o kesev o ez]"
- **LOGOS:** "a bull or a sheep or a goat"
- **PNEUMA:** "a calf, a lamb, or a goat"
- **Consensus:** "a bull or a sheep or a goat"
- **Basis:** *Shor* = "bull/ox" (not "calf"); *kesev* = "sheep/lamb." PNEUMA's "calf" and "lamb" introduce age-specific terms when the verse is *establishing* the minimum age for sacrifice — using infant terms here creates circularity. The generic adult terms (bull, sheep) are appropriate; the eight-day minimum age is what the verse establishes. "Bull or a sheep or a goat" (LOGOS) is correct.

---

### V. 27 — "With its mother" vs. "under its mother"

- **EMET:** "seven days under its mother [tachat immo]"
- **LOGOS:** "remain seven days with its mother"
- **PNEUMA:** "remain with its mother for seven days"
- **Consensus:** "remain seven days with its mother"
- **Basis:** *Tachat immo* = "under its mother" — literally beneath its mother (in the nursing position). EMET preserves the literal *tachat* ("under"), which evokes the nursing/sheltering posture. LOGOS/PNEUMA use "with its mother," which is the natural English rendering (ESV, NASB). The note records the literal "under" and the LXX's *hypo* ("under"). "With its mother" is the consensus as the more natural English equivalent.

---

### V. 27 — "Acceptable as a food offering" vs. "as a fire-offering"

- **EMET:** "accepted as a fire-offering [le-isheh]"
- **LOGOS:** "acceptable as a food offering"
- **PNEUMA:** "acceptable as a food offering"
- **Consensus:** "acceptable as a food offering"
- **Basis:** Consistent with the v.22 ruling on *isheh*. "Food offering" is the consensus rendering.

---

### V. 28 — "An ox or a sheep" vs. "a cow or a sheep"

- **EMET:** "an ox [shor]"
- **LOGOS:** "an ox"
- **PNEUMA:** "a cow"
- **Consensus:** "an ox"
- **Basis:** *Shor* is grammatically masculine and used generically for cattle. PNEUMA's "cow" introduces a female-specific term not in the MT. "An ox" (EMET/LOGOS) is the correct gender-neutral generic rendering.

---

### V. 28 — "And its young" vs. "and its offspring"

- **EMET:** "it and its offspring [oto ve-et-beno]"
- **LOGOS:** "and her young"
- **PNEUMA:** "along with its young"
- **Consensus:** "and its young"
- **Basis:** *Oto ve-et-beno* = "it and its son/young." The parent animal is unspecified for sex — the law applies regardless of whether the parent is cow or bull. LOGOS's "her young" introduces a female pronoun for the parent not in the MT. "Its young" (PNEUMA adjusted) is gender-neutral and accurate.

---

### V. 29 — "Thank offering" vs. "thanksgiving" / "thanksgiving offering"

- **EMET:** "thank offering [zevach todah]"
- **LOGOS:** "thanksgiving"
- **PNEUMA:** "thanksgiving offering"
- **Consensus:** "thank offering"
- **Basis:** *Zevach todah* = "sacrifice of acknowledgment/thanksgiving." "Thank offering" is the established project convention for *todah*, used as the compact formal term. LOGOS's plain "thanksgiving" drops "offering" and the sacrificial-category sense. PNEUMA's "thanksgiving offering" is equivalent to "thank offering"; the consensus prefers the shorter "thank offering."

---

### V. 29 — "So that it may be accepted on your behalf" — ratson formula

- **EMET:** "for your acceptance you shall sacrifice it [li-rtsone'khem]"
- **LOGOS:** "so that you may be accepted"
- **PNEUMA:** "in a way that it will be accepted on your behalf"
- **Consensus:** "so that it may be accepted on your behalf"
- **Basis:** *Li-rtsone'khem* = "for your acceptance/favor." LOGOS's "so that you may be accepted" implies the worshiper's personal acceptance rather than the offering's liturgical acceptance (*ratson* of the sacrifice). EMET's "for your acceptance" preserves the *ratson* formula but is slightly opaque. PNEUMA's "in a way that it will be accepted" is the most explicit. The consensus: "so that it may be accepted on your behalf" — making the offering the subject of acceptance and the worshiper the beneficiary.

---

### V. 30 — "That very day" vs. "the same day"

- **EMET:** "On that very day [be-yom ha-hu] it shall be eaten"
- **LOGOS:** "It shall be eaten on the same day"
- **PNEUMA:** "It must be eaten that same day"
- **Consensus:** "It shall be eaten on the same day"
- **Basis:** *Be-yom ha-hu* = "on that day" or "on that very day" (EMET's "very" reflects the demonstrative *ha-hu*). "On the same day" (LOGOS) is the conventional English rendering for this same-day consumption rule and is clearest in context. PNEUMA's "that same day" is equivalent. "Shall be eaten" is preferred over PNEUMA's "must be eaten" — both are accurate, but "shall be eaten" maintains the formal legal register.

---

### V. 31 — Opening conjunction and verb

- **EMET:** "And you shall keep my commandments and do them"
- **LOGOS:** "So you shall keep my commandments and do them"
- **PNEUMA:** "Keep my commands and carry them out"
- **Consensus:** "You shall keep my commandments and do them"
- **Basis:** The MT's *u-shmartem et-mitsvotai va-asitem otam* has the *vav*-conjunction; LOGOS's "So" is an interpretive connector not in the MT. PNEUMA's "commands" for *mitsvot* is acceptable but "commandments" is the more formal and established term for *mitsvot* in legal discourse. PNEUMA's "carry them out" for *asitem* (plain *asah*, "to do") adds emphasis not in the MT. "You shall keep my commandments and do them" preserves the MT's two verbs.

---

### V. 32 — "That I may be sanctified" vs. "and I will be sanctified" vs. "I must be treated as holy"

- **EMET:** "and I will be sanctified [ve-niqdashhti] in the midst of the sons of Yisra'el"
- **LOGOS:** "that I may be sanctified among the sons of Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "for I must be treated as holy among the Israelites"
- **Consensus:** "that I may be sanctified in the midst of the sons of Israel"
- **Basis:** *Ve-niqdashhti* is the Nifal of *q-d-sh* — the passive/reflexive: "I shall be sanctified/hallowed." Three different renderings reflect genuine interpretive variation: (1) EMET's "and I will be sanctified" reads it as a result clause; (2) LOGOS's "that I may be sanctified" reads it as a purpose clause (the purpose of not profaning the Name); (3) PNEUMA's "I must be treated as holy" is interpretive paraphrase capturing the Niphal's nuance. The purpose-clause reading (LOGOS) is slightly preferred as it captures the human-divine interactive dimension of *kiddush ha-Shem*. *Be-tokh* ("in the midst of") is preserved over LOGOS's "among" to reflect the communal-spatial dimension. "Sons of Israel" is the project standard over PNEUMA's "Israelites."

---

### V. 32 — "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites"

- **EMET:** "sons of Yisra'el"
- **LOGOS:** "sons of Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "Israelites"
- **Consensus:** "sons of Israel"
- **Basis:** *Bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel" — the project's standard rendering for this term throughout Leviticus. PNEUMA's "Israelites" is a valid paraphrase but collapses the Hebrew kinship formula.

---

### V. 33 — "The land of Egypt" vs. "Egypt"

- **EMET:** "from the land of Egypt [me-erets Mitsrayim]"
- **LOGOS:** "of the land of Egypt"
- **PNEUMA:** "out of Egypt"
- **Consensus:** "of the land of Egypt"
- **Basis:** The MT's Exodus-identification formula always includes *erets* ("land"): *me-erets Mitsrayim* = "from the land of Egypt." PNEUMA drops *erets*. The project standard for this formulaic phrase is "the land of Egypt" in full (cf. Lev 11:45; 19:36; 25:38; 26:13). EMET/LOGOS both preserve the full formula.

---
